text:
Summarize (Orchestrating the Discussion)

Discussing Solutions and Strategies

Discuss Baruti's science grades. Ask students to analyze the percentages in the grades.

Suggested Questions

  • Baruti only missed 1 point on each assignment. Does that make all of his grades the same?
No. He only missed 1 point, but the number of points changes, so that changes his grade from assignment to assignment.
• Which of the assignments has the lowest percentage grade?
Test. 24 points out of 25 points is equivalent to 96 points out of 100, or 96%.
• Which of the assignments has the lowest percentage grade?
Homework #1. 1 point out of 2 points is equivalent to 50 points out of 100 points, or 50%.
• Baruti missed 1 point on the Test and 1 point on Homework #1. Why is there such a difference in the percentages?
In the test, each point is worth 4% of the grade. In the homework assignment, each point is worth 50% of the grade. So the homework points have more influence on the percentage grade.

This idea can be illustrated graphically using Teaching Aid 4.4A: Homework and Test Points. (Portrayal) Students might express the homework points as having more "weight" or more percentage. If we remove 1 point from the Homework 1, we lose much of the percentage. If we remove 1 point from the Test, we don't lose as much percentage.
Each point on the homework counts for 50% of the grade.
Each point on the test counts for 4% of the grade.
If Baruti gets every point except, for example, point 2, his homework percentage is 50%, and his test percentage is 96%.
• How did you compute Baruti's overall grade so far?
Answers will vary. Students should use previous strategies such as scaling ratios, tape diagrams, ratios table, etc.
• Why might percent be used for grades?
It changes every grade to an amount out of 100. That makes it easier to relate one grade to another.

In general, Baruti missed 1 point on every assignment. Thinking additively, this sounds very good. However, a ratio or percent compares the quantities multiplicatively, which means that the quantities are compared to each other. So missing 1 for every 25 points is very different than missing 1 for every 2 points. Students might think of it as 1 point spread out over 2 points makes each point very "powerful" whereas 1 point spread out over 25 points makes each point "weaker." Helping students to verbalize this multiplicative versus additive relationship is an important conversation. This conversation with Baruti, his parent, and his twin sister could assist some students to see this relationship.:

Parent: What can you tell me about your science grade?

Baruti: I am doing great! I have only missed 1 point on every assignment!

Baruti's Twin Sister (in the same class):

You did great on the last test but not so great on a couple homework assignments.

Parent: (Looks at Baruti with a questioning expression.)

Baruti: Well, those two assignments did not have many points. So when I missed 1 out of 2 points, I got a 50%. And when I missed 2 out of 3 points, I got a 67%. Because the grades are ratios, missing 1 point does not tell the whole story. You need to now two quantities and how they compare to know what the grade is.

Going Further

For an extra challenge, ask students to consider what the final grade would be if the percentages were totaled instead of points. Note that it may seem counterintuitive to students that the result is different. (Problem- Solving Environment)

  • What if the science teacher uses the percentages to find the final grade rather than the total points? How many total percentage points would Baruti have?
50 out of 100, 80 out of 100, 90 out of 100, 80 out of 100, 67 out of 100, and 96 out of 100 would give him 463 out of 600.
• Would Baruti have the same grade?
No. If we scale 463 out of 600, we get about 77.1 out of 100. His grade would be a lot lower.

Making the Mathematics Explicit

Have groups present their percentage situations then explain and justify their answers to the questions. If multiple groups worked on the same example, give them time to discuss their thinking together first before there is a whole-class conversation.

Suggested Questions

  • What did you have to make sense of in the example that you worked on from What If...? Situation A?
  • Have you heard of percents being used to answer questions like the ones you had to answer?
  • What type(s) of strategies were helpful for answering the questions?

• Answers will vary.
  • Answers will vary.
  • Answers will vary. Students might mention 100 grids, tape diagrams, double number lines, unit rates, ratio tables, or scaling ratios.

Now What Do Students Know

Ask students to reflect on the discussion and answer the Now What Do You Know? questions.

See answers to the problem for more information about the strategies and embedded mathematics.

Reflecting on Student Learning

  • Use the following questions to assess student understanding at the end of a the lesson.
    • What evidence do I have that students understand the Now What Do You Know? question?
      • Where did my students get stuck?
      • What strategies did they use?
      • What breakthroughs did my students have today?
    • How will I use this to plan for tomorrow? For the next time I teach this lesson?
    • Where will I have the opportunity to reinforce these ideas as I continue through this unit? The next unit?
